does: This paper investigates reinforcement learning from human feedback as a mechanism to improve language model reasoning, specifically comparing outcome-supervised reward models (ORMs) that evaluate final answers against process-supervised reward models (PRMs) that provide step-by-step feedback. The authors apply both approaches to mathematical reasoning tasks, evaluating performance on GSM8K for simpler problems and MATH for complex problems. A key contribution is empirical characterization of how different reward aggregation functions affect model optimization, revealing that while PRM-based fine-grained rewards improve simple mathematical reasoning, they counterintuitively degrade performance on harder problems, highlighting a critical tension in reward design for reasoning tasks.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on RLHF for language models beyond preference learning toward more structured reward modeling for reasoning. It builds on prior work applying reinforcement learning to mathematical problem-solving and addresses limitations of standard outcome-based supervision by exploring process-level feedback, connecting to research on step-by-step verification and correctness checking in language models. The paper engages with recent findings about scaling laws and model capabilities on reasoning benchmarks, and relates to broader questions about how intermediate supervision and fine-grained feedback shapes model behavior, extending methods used in instruction following and preference alignment toward domain-specific reasoning tasks.
enables: These findings open multiple research directions for improving reasoning in language models through better reward design. The surprising negative result on complex tasks motivates investigation into why fine-grained rewards can hurt harder reasoning—potentially leading to new understanding of how models learn to decompose problems. The work points toward hybrid reward schemes that adapt aggregation functions based on task complexity, toward better process-level annotations that capture reasoning quality more accurately, and toward understanding fundamental differences between how models learn from outcome versus process feedback. It also suggests future work exploring curriculum-based reward strategies and domain-specific reward modeling for specialized reasoning tasks beyond mathematics.
